Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Judith Prakash JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Goh Chan Peng

Facts.Text: From 2000-05-01, Goh Chan Peng held the position of director and chief executive officer of Beyonics Technology Ltd and Beyonics International Pte Ltd, and chief executive officer of the Beyonics Group.
On 2013-01-09, Goh Chan Peng tendered his resignation from his positions in the Beyonics Group with effect from 2013-04-30.
Between 2013-01-10 and 2013-04-30, Beyonics International Pte Ltd paid monthly salary totalling SGD 45,900 to Goh Chan Peng following his resignation.
At all material times, Goh Chan Peng was the beneficial owner of Wyser International Ltd and Wyser Capital Ltd, companies incorporated in the British Virgin Islands.

On 2012-04-05, Goh Chan Peng signed two agreements on behalf of Wyser International Ltd: one with Kodec Co Ltd and one with Nedec Co Ltd. Both agreements were backdated to 2011-11-24 and provided for payments to be made to Wyser International Ltd.
Between January 2012 and January 2013, the Beyonics Group supplied more than eight million e-coated baseplates to LND for finishing instead of finishing the baseplates itself.
In late June 2011, at his own request, Mr Goh was introduced to Stephen Hwang, CEO of the NedKo Group, who indicated that he wanted to discuss doing business with Seagate.

Around mid-October 2011, in communications with Seagate, Mr Goh under-represented the Beyonics Group's capacity to produce baseplates and informed Billy Chua that the Beyonics Group was only able to continue with the supply of three million Brinks 2H baseplates per quarter.
Beyonics-I paid $99,598.80 for wine purchases made between June 2006 and December 2012.
Beyonics-I paid $1,679 being the cost of a camera lens purchased on 2 November 2012.
Beyonics-I paid $16,000 being the cost of ten fountain pens purchased on 7 December 2011.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director/CEO breaches fiduciary duties by orchestrating a business collaboration that diverts opportunities to a third-party group in exchange for personal commissions through a related entity, and by applying corporate funds for personal expenditures.
Party_Details.Rule: A director must act bona fide in the company's best interests, avoid unauthorized conflicts of interest (no-conflict rule), and refrain from obtaining personal profits from their office (no-profit rule). Corporate expenditure must be reasonably incidental to the business and for the company's benefit. Variations to employment contracts, such as notice periods and associated pay, require valid consideration.
Party_Details.Application: Goh Chan Peng, while CEO of Beyonics, initiated a collaboration with the NedKo Group. During this process, he under-represented Beyonics' capacity to Seagate and facilitated a structure where high-volume work was directed to NedKo. Concurrently, he caused his personal BVI entity, Wyser International, to enter into backdated agreements with the NedKo partners for commissions linked to those volumes. Separately, Beyonics funds were used for personal items including high-value wine, medical devices, and family course fees, and he received salary after resigning under a notice arrangement that provided no corresponding benefit to the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Using a fiduciary office to secure personal commissions from a company's commercial partners represents a fundamental conflict of interest and an unauthorized profit. Such arrangements, when not disclosed to or approved by the board, violate the duty of loyalty regardless of the director's subjective belief. Personal expenditures unrelated to corporate operations do not meet the criteria for legitimate business expenses, and the burden remains on the director to justify such use of funds. Although breaches of fiduciary duty do not automatically negate contractual salary, a specific post-resignation pay arrangement that varies the notice period in favor of the employee lacks enforceability if it is unsupported by consideration.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Judith Prakash JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wyser International Ltd

Facts.Text: Wyser International Ltd is a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands and at all material times was beneficially owned by Goh Chan Peng.
On 2012-04-05, Wyser International Ltd, through Goh Chan Peng, entered into a written agreement with Kodec Co Ltd that was dated 2011-11-24 and provided for payment of US$0.02 per e-coated baseplate.

On 2012-04-05, Wyser International Ltd, through Goh Chan Peng, entered into a written agreement with Nedec Co Ltd that was dated 2011-11-24 and provided for a payment of US$500,000 to Wyser International Ltd for assistance in securing a US$2.5 million grant funded by Seagate.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a corporate vehicle is liable to account for unauthorized profits received via commissions tied to a primary company's business while its controller holds a fiduciary office at that primary company.
Party_Details.Rule: A corporate entity is liable for knowing receipt or to account for profits if it receives funds resulting from a breach of fiduciary duty by its controller. Agreements providing for payments tied to corporate opportunities or grants associated with another company require full disclosure and authorization from that other company's board.
Party_Details.Application: Wyser International Ltd, beneficially owned by Beyonics' CEO, entered into agreements for commissions and grant-related payments with Beyonics' collaboration partners. These payments were tied directly to the volumes of baseplates produced under the Beyonics-Seagate program. The arrangements were backdated and occurred without the informed consent or knowledge of the Beyonics board.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Agreements that result in a director's personal entity receiving income tied to their primary company's business activities constitute secret profit arrangements. The recipient entity is accountable for such funds as they are obtained by reason of the fiduciary office and through a conflict of interest. The lack of transparency regarding the commission structure confirms that the payments represent unauthorized profits. Consequently, the entity is jointly and severally liable with its controller to disgorge the proceeds to the primary company.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Judith Prakash JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Beyonics Technology Ltd

Facts.Text: From 1987, the precision engineering division of the Beyonics Group, which included Beyonics Technology Ltd and its subsidiaries, supplied baseplates for hard disk drives to Seagate Technology International.
From 2000-05-01, Beyonics Technology Ltd employed Goh Chan Peng as a director and chief executive officer.
On 10 January 2012, a contract formalised an alliance between Beyonics and NedKo, where BTEC would supply e-coated baseplates to LND for finishing and sale to Seagate.
From 2012 onwards, the volume of the Beyonics Group's sales of baseplates to Seagate declined steadily.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a holding company has the standing to recover loss of profits arising from the diversion of business opportunities where the underlying contracts were performed and invoiced by its subsidiary.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the principle of separate legal personality, each company in a corporate group is a distinct entity. A holding company cannot claim for losses suffered by its subsidiary (reflective loss), even if it prepares consolidated accounts. Standing to recover profit from a contract resides with the specific contracting legal entity.
Party_Details.Application: Beyonics Technology Ltd, the holding company, maintained that its CEO breached duties by diverting the Seagate baseplate business. However, the contracts with Seagate were held by a subsidiary, Beyonics Asia Pacific Limited, which recognized the revenue. The damages sought for diversion and total loss were based on the profits that would have accrued to the subsidiary's accounts.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The doctrine of separate legal personality precludes a parent company from recovering damages for a wrong done to its subsidiary. Consolidated financial reporting does not merge distinct legal personalities into a single economic entity for the purpose of claiming contract-based profits. In the absence of a direct loss to the holding company's own independent assets, claims for lost profits belonging to its subsidiary are not actionable by the parent. Therefore, while a breach of duty may be established, the parent company lacks the standing to recover the specific profit loss suffered at the subsidiary level.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Judith Prakash JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Beyonics International Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: On 31 August 2005, Goh Chan Peng's employment agreement with Beyonics International Pte Ltd contained a contractual notice provision of six months' written notice.
From 26 July 2012 to 2 January 2013, Beyonics International Pte Ltd paid $10,329 from petty cash for treatments administered to Mr Goh by a Japanese practitioner of alternative healthcare.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company is entitled to recover corporate funds applied for the personal items of its CEO and salary paid under a resignation arrangement that lacked valid consideration.
Party_Details.Rule: Corporate expenditure must be reasonably incidental to the company's business and for its benefit. The burden of proving the business nature of an expense shifts to the director once it is shown to be prima facie personal. A variation to an employment contract, such as shortening a notice period with full pay, requires additional consideration to be enforceable.
Party_Details.Application: Beyonics International paid for its CEO's personal alternative healthcare, medical devices, and family course fees. These items were not documented as serving any corporate policy. Furthermore, following the CEO's resignation, the company paid several months of salary under a shortened notice arrangement that was requested by the employee and offered no reciprocal benefit to the employer.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Expenditures for a director's personal medical treatments and family education are not reasonably incidental to the operation of a manufacturing business. In the absence of documentation linking these costs to corporate benefit, they constitute an unauthorized application of company assets. Similarly, an agreement to pay salary for a period where no service is required, and which varies the notice period solely for the employee's benefit, is unenforceable due to a lack of consideration. Consequently, the company is entitled to restitution for the unjustified expenses and the post-resignation salary payments.
